title: 4 2 Polar Coordinates
body:
4.2 DOUBLE INTEGRALS IN POLAR COORDINATES

To transform from polar coordinates (r,őł) to Cartesian coordinates (x,y), use

x = r cos őł, y = r sin őł.

To transform from Cartesian coordinates (x,y) to polar coordinates (r,őł), use p y r= x2 + y 2 , őł = tan‚ąí1 . x The infinitesimal area in polar coordinates is

dA = r dr dőł

Sometimes integrals become much simpler when expressed in terms of the polar coordinates (r, őł) rather than the usual Cartesian coordinates (x, y). This is particularly true when dealing with circular objects or sums of squares. Let us begin by defining polar coordinates and proving the transformations above.

Jacobian Transformation (Optional)

The equation dA = dxdy = rdrdőł is a special case of a Jacobian transformation. If x = x(u, v), y = y(u, v) is a change of variable in a double integral then Z Z Z Z f (x, y)dx dy = f (x(u, v), y(u, v)) |J|du dv ‚Ą¶ ‚Ą¶‚ąó

where ‚Ą¶‚ąó is the region in the (u, v) plane corresponding to ‚Ą¶ in the (x, y) plane and J is the Jacobian determinant ‚ąāx ‚ąāx ‚ąāu ‚ąāv ‚ąāx ‚ąāy ‚ąāx ‚ąāy J= ‚ąāy ‚ąāy = ‚ąí . ‚ąāu ‚ąāv ‚ąāu ‚ąāv ‚ąāv ‚ąāu

For polar coordinates, x = r cos(őł) and y = r sin(őł) then:

‚ąāx ‚ąāx ‚ąāx ‚ąāy ‚ąāx ‚ąāy J= ‚ąār ‚ąāy ‚ąāőł ‚ąāy = ‚ąí = cos(őł)r cos(őł)+r sin(őł) sin(őł) = r(cos2 (őł)+sin2 (őł)) = r. ‚ąār ‚ąāőł ‚ąār ‚ąāőł ‚ąāőł ‚ąār

Hence, dA = r dr dőł.

We will not deal with triple integrals in this course, but this result is easily extended to three dimensions.

Example 8 (Optional) The spherical polar coordinates express the position of a point in terms of r, the radius, őł, the angle with the x-axis, and ŌÜ, the angle with the z-axis,

x = r cos őł sin ŌÜ, y = r sin őł sin ŌÜ, z = r cos ŌÜ.

Calculate the Jacobian determinant for this coordinate system and show that the infinitesimal volume for spherical polar coordinates is

dV = dx dy dz = r2 sin ŌÜ dr dőł dŌÜ.
